questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with two first-trimester miscarriages and a livedo reticularis rash on her legs. She has no other medical history. Her blood pressure is normal. She is not on any medication. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Recurrent first-trimester miscarriages plus livedo reticularis strongly suggests antiphospholipid syndrome (APS). SLE may cause similar features but typically also has other systemic symptoms; Behçet's includes oral/genital ulcers; Factor V Leiden causes thrombosis but not pregnancy loss or rash; chronic venous insufficiency does not involve miscarriages.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a deep vein thrombosis in her left leg following a long-haul flight. She has no personal or family history of thrombosis or bleeding. Routine clotting screen shows a prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT). Her platelet count is normal, and she has no bleeding symptoms. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A prolonged APTT in a young woman with thrombosis, no bleeding history, and normal platelet count is classic for antiphospholipid syndrome (lupus anticoagulant effect). Factor XII deficiency prolongs APTT but does not cause thrombosis; haemophilia and von Willebrand disease cause bleeding; SLE could cause APTT prolongation but less likely without other features.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with known antiphospholipid syndrome presents to the emergency department with acute onset of confusion, oliguria, and petechiae. He is on warfarin with a therapeutic INR. Examination reveals multiple livedo patches, blood pressure 150/90 mmHg, and mild jaundice. Blood tests show acute kidney injury, thrombocytopenia (35 x 10^9/L), and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia on blood film. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Catastrophic APS presents with multiorgan thrombosis, microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, and thrombocytopenia in a patient with known APS. TTP typically has more neurological involvement and less renal; HUS predominantly renal; DIC has consumptive coagulopathy; HIT requires heparin exposure and thrombosis but not microangiopathic anaemia.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and slurred speech. She has a history of migraine with aura and two episodes of deep vein thrombosis in the past. On examination, there is a livedo reticularis rash on her arms. MRI brain shows an acute ischaemic stroke. What is the single most likely underlying diagnosis?
options: Cerebral vasculitis due to systemic lupus erythematosus
questions.explanation: The combination of recurrent DVT, livedo reticularis, and ischaemic stroke in a young woman is highly suggestive of antiphospholipid syndrome. Migrainous infarction is less common and typically with prolonged aura; vasculitis would require other SLE features; venous sinus thrombosis presents differently; dissection often associated with pain or trauma.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a two-week history of involuntary movements of her arms and face. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. There is no family history of movement disorders. Neurological examination reveals chorea. Investigations including thyroid function, copper studies, and MRI brain are normal. She does have a mild thrombocytopenia (platelets 110 x 10^9/L) and a prolonged APTT. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Chorea can be a non-thrombotic manifestation of antiphospholipid syndrome, especially in young women. The presence of thrombocytopenia and prolonged APTT (lupus anticoagulant) supports APS. Huntington's would have family history; Wilson's has copper abnormalities; Sydenham's follows streptococcal infection; drug-induced is absent.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of DVT has a prolonged aPTT on routine coagulation screen. Mixing studies show no correction. What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Failure of correction in mixing studies indicates the presence of an inhibitor, most commonly lupus anticoagulant in this clinical context. Factor deficiencies correct with mixing.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with three first-trimester miscarriages has positive anticardiolipin antibodies (IgG, 50 GPL). What is the next step to confirm the diagnosis of antiphospholipid syndrome?
questions.explanation: Diagnosis requires persistent positivity on two or more occasions at least 12 weeks apart. Repeating anticardiolipin antibodies in 12 weeks is the correct next step.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with recurrent DVT and pulmonary embolism has a positive lupus anticoagulant test. To meet diagnostic criteria for antiphospholipid syndrome, what else is required?
options: A second positive lupus anticoagulant test after 12 weeks
questions.explanation: The laboratory criterion for APS requires persistent positivity of the same antibody on two occasions at least 12 weeks apart. A single positive test is insufficient.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with three early miscarriages has anticardiolipin IgG at 25 GPL. Which best describes this result in the context of antiphospholipid syndrome diagnosis?
options: Indicates need for repeat in 12 weeks
Indicates need for lupus anticoagulant testing
Not diagnostic because titre is too low
questions.explanation: The Sapporo/Sydney criteria require anticardiolipin antibodies at medium or high titre (>40 GPL for IgG). A low titre (25 GPL) does not meet the laboratory criterion.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus has no history of thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity. She has positive lupus anticoagulant and anticardiolipin antibodies on two occasions 12 weeks apart. Which of the following is required to diagnose antiphospholipid syndrome?
options: Both at least one clinical AND one laboratory criterion
Neither clinical nor laboratory criteria are needed
questions.explanation: Definite APS requires at least one clinical criterion (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity) and at least one laboratory criterion (persistent positive antiphospholipid antibody). This patient lacks a clinical event.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with an unprovoked proximal DVT. She has a positive lupus anticoagulant and moderate-titre anticardiolipin antibodies (IgG and IgM). There is no previous thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity. What is the first-line long-term anticoagulation for this patient?
questions.explanation: For first venous thromboembolism in antiphospholipid syndrome (APS), NICE recommends vitamin K antagonists (e.g., warfarin) with target INR 2–3. DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban) are not first-line because of increased thrombotic risk, especially in triple-positive APS. LMWH is reserved for pregnancy or warfarin intolerance, and higher INR targets are not used for first VTE.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with known triple-positive antiphospholipid syndrome (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, anti-β2 glycoprotein I antibodies) on warfarin (INR 2.5) presents with acute onset of widespread thrombotic microangiopathy, renal impairment, thrombocytopenia, and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia. She is diagnosed with catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome (CAPS). Which of the following treatments is CONTRAINDICATED in the acute phase?
questions.explanation: In catastrophic APS (CAPS), warfarin is contraindicated acutely because it takes days to achieve therapeutic effect and can paradoxically worsen thrombosis by depleting protein C. First-line therapy includes high-dose corticosteroids and plasma exchange (with or without IVIG), plus anticoagulation with heparin (UFH or LMWH).

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman with known antiphospholipid syndrome (positive lupus anticoagulant and anticardiolipin antibodies) has had three first-trimester miscarriages. She is now 8 weeks pregnant. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Low-dose aspirin plus prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin
questions.explanation: For pregnant women with APS and recurrent miscarriages, NICE recommends low-dose aspirin (75 mg daily) plus prophylactic LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg daily). This combination improves live birth rate. Warfarin is teratogenic and avoided in pregnancy. Therapeutic LMWH is reserved for those with previous thrombosis or on long-term anticoagulation.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with antiphospholipid syndrome (lupus anticoagulant positive, anticardiolipin antibodies at moderate titre) has no history of thrombosis or pregnancy complications. He has hypertension (controlled on ramipril) and type 2 diabetes. What is the most appropriate first-line prophylactic therapy?
questions.explanation: In asymptomatic APS with additional cardiovascular risk factors, NICE suggests considering low-dose aspirin for primary prevention of thrombosis. Warfarin and DOACs are not indicated for primary prophylaxis. Clopidogrel is not recommended in APS. No therapy would be appropriate only if no risk factors were present.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with triple-positive antiphospholipid syndrome (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, and anti-β2 glycoprotein I antibodies) and a history of ischaemic stroke is currently stable on long-term anticoagulation. Which of the following anticoagulants is CONTRAINDICATED for long-term secondary prevention in this patient?
questions.explanation: DOACs (e.g., rivaroxaban) are contraindicated in triple-positive APS due to high risk of recurrent thrombosis, as shown in trials (TRAPS, RAPS). Warfarin remains first-line. Heparins (UFH, LMWH) and fondaparinux can be used in acute settings or bridging, but not as long-term oral therapy except in pregnancy.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the early pregnancy clinic after three first-trimester miscarriages. She has no other medical history. Blood tests show a persistently positive lupus anticoagulant. She is not currently pregnant and uses contraception. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Reassure and arrange follow-up when she next conceives
Start low-dose aspirin 75 mg once daily
Check activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) to confirm the result
Start warfarin with a target INR 2–3
Refer to a rheumatologist for hydroxychloroquine
questions.explanation: This patient has obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome (APS). NICE guidance recommends low-dose aspirin for primary prevention of pregnancy complications. Warfarin is teratogenic and not used in this setting. Hydroxychloroquine is second-line if first-line fails. A confirmatory aPTT is not needed as lupus anticoagulant is established. Reassurance alone is inappropriate given the risk.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) is on warfarin for a previous deep vein thrombosis. His INR is 2.5. He attends the emergency department after a small cut on his hand that bled for a few minutes but has now stopped. He is haemodynamically stable and the wound is clean. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Check full blood count and coagulation screen
Give fresh frozen plasma to reverse anticoagulation
Reassure the patient and advise to continue warfarin as normal
Stop warfarin and give vitamin K orally
Reduce the warfarin dose to achieve a lower INR target
questions.explanation: Minor bleeding with a therapeutic INR does not require reversal. The patient should be reassured and continue warfarin. Vitamin K or FFP are reserved for major bleeding or need for urgent reversal. A coagulation screen is not indicated as bleeding is self-limiting. The INR target is appropriate for APS.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with known antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) presents to the emergency department with acute dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, and hypoxia. She is 20 weeks pregnant and has been on low-dose aspirin and therapeutic enoxaparin throughout pregnancy. A CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) confirms a segmental pulmonary embolism. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start warfarin with a target INR 2–3
Give intravenous recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (rt-PA)
questions.explanation: In this pregnant patient with APS presenting with acute PE despite therapeutic enoxaparin, intravenous unfractionated heparin is the most appropriate immediate step for rapid anticoagulation, especially if thrombolysis is considered. Enoxaparin is already being given, so switching to IV heparin is standard. Warfarin is teratogenic and not used in pregnancy. Thrombolysis might be considered if haemodynamically unstable, but the question asks for the next step. Caesarean section is not indicated.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) develops severe abdominal pain, thrombocytopenia (platelets 30×10⁹/L), microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (schistocytes on film), and acute kidney injury over 24 hours. She has a history of recurrent miscarriages and a previous DVT. She was on warfarin but had stopped it recently. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start heparin with a therapeutic dose
questions.explanation: This is catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome (CAPS), a life-threatening emergency. First-line treatment includes plasma exchange in combination with corticosteroids. Corticosteroids alone are insufficient. IVIG and rituximab are second-line. Heparin may be used but plasma exchange is the priority to remove antiphospholipid antibodies and address the microangiopathy.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) and recurrent arterial strokes despite a target INR of 2–3 on warfarin. He has no other risk factors. His INR is stable at 2.7. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Add low-dose aspirin 75 mg once daily
Insert an inferior vena cava filter
questions.explanation: For recurrent arterial thrombosis in APS despite adequate warfarin, NICE/BSH guidelines recommend adding low-dose aspirin (dual therapy). Increasing INR target is no longer recommended due to bleeding risk. Rivaroxaban is contraindicated in APS. Hydroxychloroquine is not first-line for this scenario. IVC filter is for venous, not arterial, events.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with known antiphospholipid syndrome on warfarin presents with acute onset of fever, confusion, oliguria, and digital ischaemia. Blood pressure 80/50. Which clinical finding most strongly suggests catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome rather than septic shock?
options: Multiple small-vessel occlusions on imaging (e.g., renal, cerebral)
questions.explanation: Catastrophic APS is defined by widespread small-vessel thrombosis affecting at least three organs, often in the setting of APS. While sepsis can cause multi-organ failure, CAPS is characterized by histopathological evidence of microthrombi. Livedo reticularis is common but not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old primigravida with known antiphospholipid syndrome on low-molecular-weight heparin presents at 30 weeks gestation with epigastric pain, nausea, and hypertension (160/100). Blood tests show haemoglobin 90 g/L, platelet count 100 x10^9/L, normal bilirubin. Which laboratory finding would most suggest HELLP syndrome rather than severe pre-eclampsia?
options: Microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (schistocytes on blood film)
questions.explanation: HELLP (haemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets) is distinguished from severe pre-eclampsia by evidence of microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia; schistocytes are a hallmark. While elevated AST and thrombocytopenia are present, they are also seen in severe pre-eclampsia; the presence of haemolysis with schistocytes points to HELLP.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with antiphospholipid syndrome (triple-positive anti-phospholipid antibodies) on warfarin (target INR 2-3) for a previous DVT presents with acute breathlessness and pleuritic chest pain. CTPA confirms multiple segmental pulmonary emboli. His INR is 2.8. Which finding is a red flag for treatment failure requiring escalation of therapy?
questions.explanation: Recurrent venous thromboembolism despite therapeutic INR (2-3) in a patient with APS, especially triple-positive, indicates failure of standard anticoagulation. This warrants consideration of a higher INR target (3-4) or addition of antiplatelet therapy. The other options are not specific for treatment failure.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with known antiphospholipid syndrome presents with a sudden, severe headache over 2 days, associated with nausea and photophobia. CT head is normal. Which clinical finding raises the strongest suspicion of cerebral venous sinus thrombosis?
options: Focal weakness of the left arm
questions.explanation: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST) is a dangerous complication of APS that often presents with headache and signs of raised intracranial pressure. Bilateral papilloedema is a key indicator of raised ICP from impaired venous drainage. Focal weakness or aphasia may suggest arterial stroke, but the normal CT and gradual headache make CVST more likely.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with known antiphospholipid syndrome (on warfarin for a previous pulmonary embolism) presents with a 2-month history of arthralgia, fatigue, and a facial rash. Which clinical finding would most suggest she has developed systemic lupus erythematosus rather than an isolated antiphospholipid flare?
questions.explanation: A malar rash is a classic and specific feature of SLE, occurring in up to 70% of patients. Livedo reticularis can occur in APS alone. Positive anti-cardiolipin antibodies and prolonged aPTT are common in APS as well as SLE.
